FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, N=11,066 children ages 9-10 at baseline, followed to 1-year follow-up. Prospective cohort from 21 US sites.

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying smartphone/screen time variables: texting (phone), video chat (video chat on phone), video games (gaming), television/streaming videos. Separate β coefficients reported for each modality. Applies B1 (YSTS papers reporting separate β for SM, texting, video chat, mobile phone).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between baseline screen time (exposure) and BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up (outcome). Table 2 reports β coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for each screen time modality.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients fully extractable. Table 2 reports B (β) coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values. Examples: total screen time B=0.22 (95% CI 0.10-0.34), texting B=0.92 (95% CI 0.29-1.55), video chat B=0.72 (95% CI 0.09-1.36), video games B=0.42 (95% CI 0.06-0.78).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data. Multiple linear regression analyses conducted with prospective design examining associations between baseline screen time and BMI percentile at follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Pediatric Obesity, 2021) with clear DOI. No indication of duplication with preprint, abstract, or deposit version.

FT_EC7_reason: Fails condition (a): Smartphone/SM is NOT the dependent variable. BMI percentile is the outcome; screen time is the predictor. This is a screen-time-as-IV analysis, not demographics-to-SM analysis. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Strong inclusion candidate. Contemporary screen time modalities examined with separate β per modality. Longitudinal prospective design with large diverse sample. All FT-IC criteria clearly met.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, nationwide sample of 11,875 participants (analysis on subsamples for Part 1: N=11,727 and Part 2: N=11,672) ages 9-10 years at baseline.

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen time variables examined: television/TV and movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, social media, video chat. Separate β coefficients reported for each modality in regression analyses. Applies B1 (YSTS papers reporting separate β for SM, texting, video chat, etc.).

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression analyses and correlation analyses. Paper states: 'conducting a series of correlation and regression analyses, controlling for SES and race/ethnicity.' Table 2 and Table 3 report standardized beta coefficients and t-statistics with p-values for main effects and interactions.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients fully extractable. Table 2 and Table 3 report standardized beta coefficients, t-statistics, p-values, and standard errors for multiple outcomes and predictor variables. Examples: depression main effect of screen time β=0.018 p=.214, anxiety β=-0.002 p=.877.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data. Cross-sectional regression and correlation analyses examining relationships between screen time and mental health, academic, and social outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (PLoS ONE, 2021) with DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0256591. No evidence of duplication.

FT_EC7_reason: Fails condition (a): Smartphone/SM is NOT the dependent variable. Multiple outcomes (mental health, academic, social, sleep) are the DVs; screen time is IV. This is screen-time-as-IV→outcomes analysis, not demographics-as-IV→SM. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Strong inclusion candidate. Separate coefficients reported for multiple screen time modalities (B1) including texting, video chat, social media. Large diverse ABCD sample. Comprehensive outcome variables. All FT-IC criteria clearly met.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study cohort substudy. Pilot subsample of 67 participants (ages 11-12 years at time of passive sensing period) from larger ABCD cohort. Study conducted 2019-2020.

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone variables examined: smartphone screen use overall and by app category (streaming, YouTube, communication, gaming, social media). Paper reports objective passive sensing measurements of smartphone use by time and app types. Qualifies as smartphone ownership/use measurement. Applies B1 (smartphone app/category use).

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential tests reported. Pearson correlations between passive sensing data and self/parent-reported screen time (r=0.49). Analyses of variance comparing app use by demographics. Two-sample t-tests examining differences in app use. Paper states 'Analyses of variance were run to assess differences in categorical app use by demographics.'

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric data fully extractable. Figure 1 shows correlation r=0.49 (P<.001) between passive sensing and self-report. Table 1 reports mean app use with SD for each category (e.g., streaming 1 h 57 min SD 1 h 32 min, social media 36 min SD 1 h 7 min). Demographic comparisons report F-statistics and p-values.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data. Pilot substudy with original data collection (passive sensing of smartphone use via EARS app over 4 weeks) and original analysis of app use patterns and measurement validation.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (JMIR Mental Health, 2021) with DOI 10.2196/29426. No evidence of duplication.

FT_EC7_reason: Fails condition (a): Smartphone/SM is NOT the dependent variable in external outcome prediction. This is a measurement/validation paper examining passive sensing of smartphone use. While smartphone use is the primary focus, the paper does not test SM/smartphone as DV with demographic predictors. No SM→outcome analyses. Primarily descriptive and methodological (measurement substudy). EC7 does not apply.
notes: Inclusion candidate with caveat. This is a measurement/validation substudy paper rather than a traditional outcomes analysis. Original ABCD data and original passive sensing methodology. Qualifies under B1 for smartphone app-category measurement. However, note that this is primarily a methods/validation paper (passive sensing substudy) rather than an outcomes study. Paper focuses on demonstrating feasibility and validity of passive sensing measurement rather than predicting non-SM outcomes.
